title: EVs’ Year Of Living Dangerously
gist: China's EV market is on track for its first-ever annual sales decline in 2026, yet instead of consolidating the industry is fragmenting further, with BYD and Tesla losing share to a half-dozen rivals - a dynamic sustained by exports now equal to a third of Chinese EV makers' unit sales.

insights.claim: China's domestic EV sales are on track to fall by roughly 7% in 2026, which would be the segment's first-ever annual decline.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Marks a structural inflection in the world's largest EV market; a persistent decline would pressure 2026-2027 volume and earnings estimates across Chinese auto and battery supply chains.
insights.quote: Domestic EV sales are on track to fall by -7% this year, their first-ever annual decline.
insights.locator: China's passenger car market is having a terrible 2026

insights.claim: Alongside the sales decline, pricing and margins for Chinese EVs are under pressure, pointing to an intensifying price war rather than an orderly demand slowdown.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Margin compression on top of falling volumes squeezes profitability across the sector, raising the odds of distress at weaker Chinese EV makers before any real shakeout.
insights.quote: Prices and margins are under pressure.
insights.locator: China's passenger car market is having a terrible 2026

insights.claim: Despite the shrinking domestic market, China's EV industry is not consolidating - it is fragmenting further, with long-time leaders BYD and Tesla losing share to at least half a dozen competitors that have strong technology, good designs and vertically integrated supply chains.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: The expected shakeout that would favor scale leaders like BYD and Tesla is not materializing, so investors should not assume market-share stability for incumbents even as the overall pie shrinks.
insights.quote: Long-time market leaders BYD and Tesla are losing share to at least a half-dozen competitors with strong technology, good designs and vertically integrated supply chains.
insights.locator: Yet despite all that, the market is not consolidating

insights.claim: Exports now account for about a third of Chinese EV makers' total unit sales, and this export growth is the key reason the domestic industry has avoided the consolidation that a shrinking home market would normally force.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Chinese EV makers' near-term health is increasingly hostage to foreign market access rather than domestic demand, making trade policy a first-order risk factor for the sector.
insights.quote: What makes it all possible is exports, which now account for a third of Chinese EV makers’ unit sales.
insights.locator: What makes it all possible is exports

insights.claim: The critical open question for the sector is how long international markets will keep absorbing the rising volume of Chinese EV exports.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Watch for tariff or trade-defense escalation in export destination markets as the likely trigger that could finally force the long-delayed consolidation in China's EV industry.
insights.quote: The question is how long international markets will be willing to absorb the rising tide of Chinese imports.
insights.locator: The question is how long international markets
